question: Explain the ACID properties of a transaction with an example of a bank fund transfer.
expectedPoints: Atomicity: Either both debit and credit happen or neither happens
Consistency: After transaction, total money in system remains same
Isolation: Concurrent transfers don't interfere with each other
Durability: Once committed, record persists even after system crash
Example: Transfer Rs 1000 from account A to B - both operations must succeed together

question: Draw and explain the transaction state diagram.
expectedPoints: Five states: Active, Partially Committed, Committed, Failed, Aborted
Active: Transaction executing
Partially Committed: Last statement executed, waiting for completion
Committed: Successfully completed, changes permanent
Failed: Cannot proceed normally
Abrolled: Rolled back to original state

question: What is Serializability? Differentiate between Conflict Serializability and View Serializability.
expectedPoints: Serializability: Concurrent schedule producing same result as some serial schedule
Conflict Serializability: Can be transformed to serial schedule by swapping non-conflicting operations
View Serializability: Same read operations and final writes as some serial schedule
Every conflict-serializable is view-serializable, but not vice versa
Conflict serializability is easier to test (precedence graph)

question: Explain the concept of conflicting operations with examples.
expectedPoints: Conflicting operations: Different transactions, same data item, at least one is write
Three types: Read-Write, Write-Read, Write-Write
Example: T1:Write(X), T2:Read(X) - Write-Read conflict
Example: T1:Read(X), T2:Write(X) - Read-Write conflict
Example: T1:Write(X), T2:Write(X) - Write-Write conflict

question: What is a Precedence Graph? How is it used to test serializability?
expectedPoints: Precedence Graph (Serialization Graph) is a directed graph
Nodes represent transactions in the schedule
Edge from Ti to Tj exists if Tj reads/writes X after Ti writes X
If graph has NO cycle: schedule is conflict-serializable
If graph HAS cycle: schedule is NOT conflict-serializable

question: Define the following: (i) Recoverable Schedule (ii) Cascadeless Schedule
expectedPoints: Recoverable Schedule: If T2 reads value written by T1, then T1 must commit before T2 commits
Cascadeless Schedule: If T2 reads value written by T1, then T1 must commit before T2 reads (not just before T2 commits)
Cascadeless is stricter than recoverable
Cascadeless prevents cascading rollbacks

question: Consider the following schedule: T1: R(X), W(X); T2: R(X), W(X). Is this schedule serializable? Use precedence graph to justify.
expectedPoints: Operations: T1:R(X), T1:W(X), T2:R(X), T2:W(X)
T1 writes X before T2 reads X - edge T1→T2
T2 writes X after T1 writes X - edge T1→T2 again
Only edge: T1→T2
No cycle exists
Schedule is serializable (equivalent to T1 then T2)
